manifest.title: Can Discus Find a Market?
manifest.description: Competitive pricing, positioning, and an honest marketability assessment for the Discus MVP.

charts.title: Verified entry prices
charts.subtitle: Public USD monthly price or monthly equivalent on annual billing, checked July 16, 2026

tables.title: Comparable services
tables.subtitle: Direct competitors and substitutes, using current public product and pricing pages

tables.title: Where Discus can and cannot compete
tables.subtitle: Product evidence from the current MVP compared with the jobs competitors emphasize

blocks.body:
## Executive Summary

- **Yes, Discus is potentially marketable—but as a narrow niche service, not yet as a broad streaming platform.** The strongest proposition is a disposable, invite-only listening room for a live DJ set: one browser link for the DJ, one for listeners, no listener accounts, and no video overhead.
- **The market is validated but intensely price-competitive.** Mixlr offers a free three-hour tier, Livesets and SonoBus are free, and Audiomovers LISTENTO starts at a $4.99 monthly equivalent on annual billing. Discus cannot win on generic audio quality or low price alone.
- **The biggest strategic constraint is music licensing, not code.** An expiring or unlisted link is an access control; it is not itself a license for streaming copyrighted recordings. Discus should initially avoid promising public fan broadcasting or takedown protection and obtain specialist legal advice before commercial launch.
- **The realistic next move is a paid concierge pilot, not a full SaaS launch.** Test $5 event passes and a $9-$12 founder subscription with small Discord-based DJ collectives, then proceed only if repeat use and payment behavior are real.

## Discus is selling privacy and immediacy, not a radio station

The current MVP captures a DJ's mixer or interface in Chrome or Edge, publishes **192 kbps stereo Opus over WebRTC**, and gives invited listeners a browser player. The owner creates separate expiring DJ and listener links; the product tracks live and historical listeners, reconnects after interruptions, supports listener re-sharing, and can announce the session to Discord. The configured ceiling is **20 concurrent listeners**.

That bundle is meaningfully simpler than an internet-radio stack and more audience-friendly than a pro-audio collaboration tool. Its most defensible promise is: **“Open a private room, connect your mixer, and share one listening link.”**

## Free and low-cost substitutes make generic pricing difficult

The chart uses only prices that were directly visible on official pages. It is not an apples-to-apples value comparison: free plans have limits, annual prices require prepayment, and radio products include much more infrastructure. It does show the commercial problem clearly—**a DJ can already try several credible alternatives for $0, while professional low-latency audio is available for under $10 per month on annual billing.**

Therefore, Discus should charge for a workflow competitors do not package as cleanly: a small, temporary, invite-only DJ room with a frictionless listener experience.

## Competitors sell scale, professional fidelity, community, or legality

No single service exactly matches Discus, but each owns a stronger adjacent promise. Mixlr sells effortless professional broadcasting and audience scale. Audiomovers sells lossless, ultra-low-latency professional collaboration. Mixcloud sells a licensed creator ecosystem with monetization. Livesets sells a free DJ-specific community and says it reports tracks and pays performance-rights organizations. SonoBus sells free, open-source technical control. Radio.co sells a complete station business.

**The open lane is smaller:** private social listening for people who do not want a station, creator profile, DAW workflow, installed client, or Discord voice channel.

## The honest verdict: promising utility, unproven business

**Discus solves a real problem and is coherent enough to demo and charge for experimentally.** Its setup flow, role-specific links, private access, listener experience, and Discord announcement are more product-like than a technical prototype. A buyer can understand the outcome in one sentence.

**It is not broadly market-ready.** The MVP has one concurrent room, a 20-listener ceiling, no billing, no customer account model, no multi-tenant isolation story, no public reliability record or service-level commitment, no rights-management system, no moderation or abuse workflow, and limited post-session analytics. Self-hosting on one small server is suitable for pilot learning, not a commercial reliability claim.

The likely business is a **small profitable niche or feature**, not a venture-scale standalone platform, unless customer discovery reveals a larger private-event use case outside copyrighted DJ sets. That is still worthwhile—but it argues for disciplined validation before major engineering.

## Run a paid pilot before building more platform

1. **Choose one beachhead:** private Discord-based DJ collectives hosting remote sessions for 5-20 invited listeners. Do not market to every broadcaster.
2. **Test two prices:** a $5 single-session pass and a $9-$12 monthly founder plan. Include a limited free trial, but require real payment during the pilot. These are test prices, not a final pricing recommendation.
3. **Use a concrete 30-session gate:** recruit 10-15 DJs or collectives and observe at least 30 live sessions. Continue only if at least 50% of activated hosts run a second session within 30 days, at least 25% pay at one test price, at least 90% of sessions start successfully, median DJ setup is under three minutes, and hands-on support stays below ten minutes per session.
4. **Build only what blocks conversion:** customer accounts and billing, room-level controls, revoke/rotate links, basic branding, stream-health diagnostics, clearer mobile/browser support, and a documented privacy/abuse process.
5. **Treat licensing as a launch gate:** have counsel assess the intended countries, public-performance implications, platform liability, required terms, takedown process, and whether the exact invite-only use case changes any obligations.

## Questions that should decide the product direction

- Do hosts value **no listener account** enough to pay, or will they default to Discord, Mixlr, or Mixcloud?
- Is the buyer the DJ, the collective/community owner, a promoter, or a private-event host?
- Is 500 ms of listener buffering acceptable for the intended social experience, and is 192 kbps stereo audibly sufficient on real listener devices?
- Can a legally supportable use case be defined tightly enough to market without implying that private links solve music rights?
- Does demand remain when the founder is not personally helping with setup?

## Caveats and assumptions

This is a July 16, 2026 desk-research assessment using the current local MVP and official public competitor pages. It does not include customer interviews, traffic estimates, conversion data, support burden, measured infrastructure costs, or a legal opinion. Mixcloud's public feature page confirmed its Pro subscription structure and licensed/private streaming features but did not expose a reliable current USD checkout price, so no Mixcloud dollar figure is quoted. Recommended prices and pilot thresholds are decision rules proposed for validation, not observed market benchmarks.

competitors.fit: Private, high-quality audio links for professional review and collaboration
competitors.price: Basic $12.99 monthly or $59.99/year; Pro $24.99 monthly or $119.99/year; Business $275/year
competitors.angle: Lossless, low-latency, multichannel audio for music professionals; 10 Basic or 150 Pro simultaneous listeners
competitors.implication: The toughest quality and price anchor. Discus must beat it on DJ-room simplicity, not fidelity.

competitors.fit: Free live and recorded DJ-set broadcasting
competitors.angle: DJ-only discovery community, built-in DJ-software broadcasting, tracklists, recordings, and royalty reporting
competitors.implication: Directly owns the free DJ niche, but is public/community-oriented rather than disposable and private.

competitors.fit: Public or unlisted live DJ streaming with creator tools
competitors.price: Paid Pro subscription; current USD checkout price was not reliably exposed on the public page
competitors.angle: Licensed streaming without takedowns, royalties to artists, community, uploads, tips, subscriptions, and analytics
competitors.implication: A stronger choice for public audience growth and rights coverage; Discus should not compete on those claims.

competitors.fit: Simple live-audio events with private access and listener analytics
competitors.price: Beginner free for 3 live hours/month; Intermediate $49 monthly or $38/month billed annually; Advanced $150 monthly or $83/month annually
competitors.angle: Effortless professional live audio, unlimited audience scale, branded pages, archives, and reliability
competitors.implication: Closest broad competitor. Discus can be smaller, more private, more DJ-specific, and cheaper—but less proven.

competitors.fit: Full internet-radio station creation and operation
competitors.price: Lite $35/month; Standard $59; Plus $139; Premium $199; optional add-ons
competitors.angle: Launch and grow a station with scheduling, storage, apps, roles, analytics, players, and large audiences
competitors.implication: Overkill for Discus's use case and proof that complete station operations support much higher pricing.

competitors.angle: Technical control, peer-to-peer quality, effects, recording, and cross-platform apps/plugins
competitors.implication: A powerful free substitute for technical users, but app installation and complex controls leave room for a simpler audience experience.

positioning.current: 20-listener configured ceiling and one private relay at a time
positioning.market_effect: Suitable for pilots and intimate rooms; not competitive for public broadcasts or large events.

positioning.current: Chrome or Edge, HTTPS, mixer/interface input, device selection, stereo meter, five-second test
positioning.market_effect: Strong usability wedge, though browser and audio-routing edge cases will create support load.

positioning.current: Expiring listener links, listener re-sharing, optional Discord live announcement
positioning.market_effect: Good fit for private communities; lacks discovery, persistent channels, embeds, and follower growth.

positioning.current: Browser link with no listener account or installed client
positioning.market_effect: The clearest differentiation against professional collaboration apps and community platforms.

positioning.current: WebRTC reconnect, 60-second DJ grace period, 500 ms listener jitter target, self-hosted deployment
positioning.market_effect: Promising mechanics, but no external uptime, device-coverage, or load evidence yet.

positioning.current: Private access controls; no music licensing, content identification, royalty reporting, moderation, or takedown workflow
positioning.market_effect: Material launch risk for commercial DJ streaming; privacy must not be marketed as licensing.

positioning.current: Stereo Opus targeted at 192 kbps and 48 kHz capture
positioning.market_effect: Credible listening quality, but not a defensible lead against lossless pro-audio tools.
